Name: Sulfuric acid
Appearance: colorless to slightly yellow oily liquid

Toxicity.Effect: corrosive burns; respiratory damage from vapor/mist
Toxicity.AntidoteNote: irrigate with water; no chemical antidote

Notes: dilution is strongly exothermic — add acid to water, never the reverse

Name: Hydrochloric acid
Appearance: colorless to faint yellow fuming liquid (aqueous)

Toxicity.Effect: skin/eye burns; respiratory irritation from HCl gas

Notes: aqueous solution of hydrogen chloride gas; stomach acid component

Toxicity.Effect: yellow staining burns (xanthoproteic); NOx inhalation injury
Toxicity.DurationNote: immediate; pulmonary edema may be delayed

Name: Phosphoric acid

Toxicity.Effect: skin and eye irritation/burns

Notes: food-grade used as acidulant in colas

Name: Acetic acid
Appearance: colorless liquid with pungent vinegar odor

Toxicity.Effect: glacial form burns skin; dilute (vinegar) is edible

Notes: vinegar is ~4-8% acetic acid

Name: Citric acid

Toxicity.Effect: mild eye/skin irritation; edible in food amounts

Notes: weak acid abundant in citrus; common chelator and preservative

Name: Carbonic acid
Appearance: exists only in solution (CO2 in water)
Notes: unstable; equilibrium of dissolved CO2; gives carbonated water its acidity

Name: Hydrofluoric acid

Toxicity.Effect: deep, often painless burns; fluoride causes systemic hypocalcemia and cardiac arrhythmia
Toxicity.DurationNote: burns progress for hours; small skin exposures can be fatal

Notes: dissolves glass; one of the most dangerous common lab acids

Name: Hydrocyanic acid
Appearance: colorless volatile liquid/gas, faint bitter-almond odor

Toxicity.Effect: blocks cytochrome c oxidase → histotoxic hypoxia

Notes: weak acid; the molecular form of cyanide; industrial and combustion hazard

Name: Boric acid

Toxicity.Effect: GI upset; chronic exposure toxic; insecticidal

Notes: mild antiseptic and insecticide

Name: Formic acid

Toxicity.Effect: burns; metabolic acidosis if ingested

Notes: simplest carboxylic acid; ant/sting venom component

Name: Oxalic acid

Toxicity.Effect: binds calcium → hypocalcemia; kidney stones/failure (oxalate)

Notes: found in rhubarb leaves; rust/stain remover

Name: Lactic acid

Toxicity.Effect: mild irritation; metabolite in the body

Notes: produced by muscle metabolism and fermentation

Name: Perchloric acid

Toxicity.Effect: corrosive burns; strong oxidizer, explosion risk with organics

Notes: one of the strongest acids; concentrated/hot form is a powerful, dangerous oxidizer

Name: Sulfurous acid

Toxicity.Effect: respiratory irritation (SO2); mucous-membrane irritation

Notes: dissolved SO2 in water; unstable

Name: Nitrous acid

Toxicity.Effect: forms carcinogenic nitrosamines

Notes: exists only in cold dilute solution; used in diazotization

Name: Sodium hydroxide

Toxicity.Effect: liquefactive necrosis; deep burns; eye damage

Notes: lye/caustic soda; used in soap-making and drain cleaner

Name: Potassium hydroxide

Toxicity.Effect: severe burns; eye damage

Notes: caustic potash; soft-soap and electrolyte use

Name: Calcium hydroxide

Toxicity.Effect: alkaline irritation/burns; respiratory irritation

Notes: slaked lime; mortar, water treatment, 'limewater'

Name: Ammonia

Toxicity.Effect: airway and mucous-membrane burns; pulmonary edema at high levels

Notes: density given as gas at STP; fertilizer feedstock and refrigerant; colorless (white hex is placeholder)

Name: Ammonium hydroxide

Toxicity.Effect: irritant/corrosive to eyes, skin, airways

Notes: aqueous ammonia ('household ammonia' when dilute)

Name: Magnesium hydroxide

Toxicity.Effect: mild laxative/antacid; large doses cause diarrhea

Notes: milk of magnesia

Name: Aluminium hydroxide

Toxicity.Effect: antacid; generally well tolerated

Notes: amphoteric; antacid and flame retardant

Name: Lithium hydroxide

Toxicity.Effect: caustic burns

Notes: CO2 scrubber in spacecraft/submarines; battery and grease use

Name: Barium hydroxide

Toxicity.Effect: caustic burns; soluble barium is a muscle/cardiac toxin
Toxicity.AntidoteNote: irrigation; ingestion: sulfate salts to precipitate barium

Notes: soluble barium compounds are toxic, unlike insoluble barium sulfate

Name: Sodium chloride

Toxicity.Effect: hypernatremia/dehydration in massive excess

Notes: table/rock salt; essential electrolyte; food preservation

Name: Potassium chloride

Toxicity.PotencyNote: low orally; toxic IV in bolus
Toxicity.Effect: cardiac arrest if rapidly infused (hyperkalemia)

Notes: salt substitute; fertilizer (potash)

Name: Potassium nitrate

Toxicity.Effect: GI irritation; methemoglobinemia in large doses

Notes: saltpeter; oxidizer in black powder, fertilizer, food curing

Name: Sodium nitrate

Toxicity.Effect: methemoglobinemia; nitrosamine precursor

Notes: Chile saltpeter; fertilizer and curing agent

Name: Ammonium nitrate

Toxicity.Effect: irritant; methemoglobinemia in excess

Notes: major fertilizer and a strong oxidizer; detonable when contaminated/confined (industrial disaster history)

Name: Ammonium chloride

Toxicity.Effect: acidosis/GI upset in large doses

Notes: sal ammoniac; flux, electrolyte, expectorant

Name: Ammonium sulfate

Toxicity.Effect: mild irritant

Notes: widely used nitrogen fertilizer

Name: Calcium carbonate
Notes: limestone, chalk, marble, shells; antacid and building material

Name: Sodium carbonate

Toxicity.Effect: eye/skin/GI irritation

Notes: washing soda / soda ash; glassmaking, cleaning, pH control

Name: Sodium bicarbonate
Notes: baking soda; mild antacid, leavening, fire suppression

Name: Potassium carbonate

Toxicity.Effect: irritation/burns

Notes: potash; glass, soap, and food (pretzels) use

Name: Calcium sulfate
Notes: gypsum/plaster of Paris/drywall; desiccant; tofu coagulant

Name: Magnesium sulfate

Toxicity.Effect: laxative; large IV doses cause hypotension/CNS depression

Notes: Epsom salt; bath soak, laxative, eclampsia treatment

Name: Copper(II) sulfate
Appearance: blue crystalline solid (pentahydrate); white when anhydrous

Toxicity.Effect: GI corrosion, hemolysis, liver/kidney injury in quantity

Notes: blue vitriol; fungicide, algaecide, electroplating

Name: Iron(II) sulfate

Toxicity.Effect: GI corrosion, metabolic acidosis, liver injury (iron toxicity)

Notes: copperas/green vitriol; iron supplement, water treatment

Name: Zinc sulfate

Toxicity.Effect: GI irritation/emetic; excess impairs copper absorption

Notes: dietary zinc source, mordant, wood preservative

Name: Calcium chloride

Toxicity.Effect: irritation; concentrated solution mildly corrosive

Notes: de-icer, desiccant, road dust control; dissolution releases heat

Name: Magnesium chloride

Toxicity.Effect: laxative effect in excess

Notes: de-icer, tofu coagulant (nigari), dust control

Name: Potassium permanganate

Toxicity.Effect: corrosive oxidizer; GI burns, airway edema if concentrated

Notes: strong oxidizer; disinfectant, water treatment, survival fire-starting/antiseptic

Name: Sodium thiosulfate
Notes: photographic fixer ('hypo'); chlorine neutralizer; itself a cyanide antidote

Name: Silver nitrate

Toxicity.Effect: caustic; chronic exposure causes argyria (grey skin)

Notes: cauterizing agent, antiseptic, photography; stains skin black

Name: Sodium sulfate

Toxicity.Effect: osmotic laxative in quantity

Notes: Glauber's salt; detergents, glassmaking, thermal storage

Name: Sodium fluoride

Toxicity.Effect: GI distress, hypocalcemia, cardiac effects; chronic excess → fluorosis

Notes: water fluoridation, toothpaste, pesticide

Name: Borax

Toxicity.Effect: irritation; reproductive toxicant in high chronic doses

Notes: sodium tetraborate decahydrate (the common household 'borax'); cleaning, flux, slime, insecticide

Name: Sodium hypochlorite

Toxicity.Effect: corrosive; mixing with acid/ammonia releases chlorine/chloramine gas

Notes: liquid bleach (~3-8%); disinfectant; never mix with ammonia or acids

Name: Calcium phosphate
Notes: main mineral of bone and teeth; fertilizer feedstock

Name: Potassium iodide

Toxicity.Effect: iodism/thyroid effects with excess

Notes: thyroid-blocking agent in radiation emergencies; dietary iodine, expectorant

Name: Sodium nitrite

Toxicity.Effect: methemoglobinemia (impairs O2 transport); hypotension

Notes: meat curing (anti-botulinum), corrosion inhibitor; also a cyanide antidote

Name: Potassium chlorate

Toxicity.Effect: methemoglobinemia, hemolysis, kidney injury

Notes: strong oxidizer; matches, pyrotechnics, oxygen candles; explosive with fuels/friction

Name: Ammonium perchlorate

Toxicity.Effect: perchlorate disrupts thyroid iodine uptake

Notes: solid-rocket-propellant oxidizer; powerful oxidizer

Name: Lead(II) nitrate

Toxicity.Effect: lead poisoning: neurotoxicity, anemia, kidney/GI effects

Notes: soluble lead salt; historically a mordant and oxidizer

Name: Barium sulfate
Notes: radiocontrast 'barium meal'; insolubility makes it non-toxic where soluble barium is poisonous

Name: Potassium dichromate

Toxicity.Effect: hexavalent chromium: corrosive, ulcers, carcinogenic, kidney/liver damage
Toxicity.AntidoteNote: irrigation; supportive; ascorbic acid reduces Cr(VI)

Notes: Cr(VI) oxidizer; tanning, analytical reagent; significant carcinogen

Name: Calcium hypochlorite

Toxicity.Effect: corrosive; releases chlorine gas with acid

Notes: 'pool chlorine'/bleaching powder; water disinfection

Name: Sodium silicate
Appearance: colorless to white glassy solid or syrupy solution

Toxicity.Effect: irritant/corrosive to eyes and skin

Notes: water glass; adhesive, sealant, egg preservation, detergent builder

Name: Water

Toxicity.PotencyNote: essential to life; non-toxic (water intoxication only in extreme excess)

Notes: the universal solvent; colorless (hex is a faint placeholder tint)

Name: Carbon dioxide

Toxicity.Effect: displaces oxygen; >~10% causes unconsciousness

Notes: density as gas at STP; respiration/combustion product; dry ice when solid; colorless

Name: Carbon monoxide

Toxicity.Effect: binds hemoglobin (carboxyhemoglobin) → tissue hypoxia

Notes: product of incomplete combustion; odorless makes it especially dangerous; colorless

Name: Silicon dioxide

Toxicity.Effect: silicosis and lung cancer from inhaled crystalline silica dust

Notes: quartz, sand, glass; bulk solid is inert, the hazard is fine dust

Name: Iron(III) oxide
Notes: rust/hematite; pigment (red ochre), thermite component

Name: Iron(II,III) oxide
Notes: magnetite/lodestone; ferromagnetic; pigment and magnetic media

Name: Aluminium oxide

Toxicity.Effect: mild respiratory irritation from dust

Notes: alumina/corundum; abrasive, refractory, ruby/sapphire host, aluminium ore intermediate

Name: Calcium oxide

Toxicity.Effect: alkaline burns; reacts with skin moisture releasing heat

Notes: quicklime; cement, steelmaking flux; violent reaction with water makes slaked lime

Name: Magnesium oxide

Toxicity.PotencyNote: low; metal-fume fever from fresh fume
Toxicity.Effect: fume fever (flu-like) from inhaling fresh MgO fume

Notes: magnesia; refractory, antacid, supplement

Name: Sulfur dioxide

Toxicity.Effect: bronchoconstriction, airway/eye irritation, pulmonary edema

Notes: density as gas at STP; volcanic/combustion gas, preservative, acid-rain precursor

Name: Sulfur trioxide

Toxicity.Effect: violently forms sulfuric acid on contact with moisture → severe burns

Notes: reacts with water to make sulfuric acid; oleum component

Name: Nitrogen dioxide

Toxicity.Effect: deep-lung irritant; delayed pulmonary edema

Notes: brown component of smog/NOx; oxidizer; delayed lung injury is characteristic

Name: Nitrous oxide

Toxicity.Effect: euphoria/anesthesia; oxygen deprivation; chronic use → B12/nerve damage

Notes: 'laughing gas'; anesthetic, propellant, engine oxidizer; supports combustion

Name: Zinc oxide

Toxicity.PotencyNote: low; fresh fume causes metal-fume fever
Toxicity.Effect: fume fever from welding/galvanizing fumes

Notes: sunscreen, pigment (zinc white), rubber, skin protectant

Name: Titanium dioxide

Toxicity.PotencyNote: low; possible carcinogen as fine dust
Toxicity.Effect: respiratory effects from prolonged dust inhalation

Notes: the standard white pigment; paint, sunscreen, food coloring (E171)

Name: Copper(II) oxide

Toxicity.Effect: GI irritation; metal-fume fever

Notes: black copper oxide; pigment, catalyst, ore

Name: Hydrogen peroxide

Toxicity.Effect: bleaching of skin; concentrated causes burns and embolism if ingested

Notes: strong oxidizer; antiseptic (3%), bleach, rocket monopropellant (high %)

Name: Lead(II) oxide

Toxicity.Effect: lead poisoning: neuro/hematologic/renal

Notes: litharge; lead glass, ceramics, batteries, historic paint

Name: Manganese dioxide

Toxicity.Effect: manganism (neurological) from chronic dust/fume inhalation

Notes: dry-cell battery cathode, catalyst, glass decolorizer, pigment

Name: Chromium(III) oxide
Notes: green pigment (chrome green), abrasive (green rouge); Cr(III) is far less toxic than Cr(VI)

Name: Lead dioxide

Toxicity.Effect: lead poisoning; strong oxidizer

Notes: positive plate in lead-acid batteries; oxidizer

Name: Tin(IV) oxide
Notes: cassiterite (tin ore); ceramic glaze opacifier, polishing powder, gas sensors

Name: Dinitrogen tetroxide
Appearance: colorless to pale-brown liquid in equilibrium with NO2

Toxicity.Effect: like NO2: severe lung irritant, delayed edema

Notes: storable rocket oxidizer (with hydrazine fuels); equilibrium with NO2

Name: Carbon steel
Notes: iron + ~0.05-2% carbon; the workhorse structural alloy; composition is nominal (mass-fraction), molar mass undefined

Name: Stainless steel
Notes: iron + ~10.5-20% Cr + Ni; passive chromium-oxide layer resists corrosion; nominal composition

Name: Cast iron
Notes: iron + ~2-4% carbon + silicon; hard, brittle, good in compression; nominal composition

Name: Brass

Toxicity.PotencyNote: low (bulk); zinc fume fever on overheating

Notes: copper + ~5-45% zinc; ratio sets color/hardness; nominal composition

Name: Bronze
Notes: copper + ~12% tin (often other elements); harder than copper; nominal composition

Name: Solder (tin-lead)

Toxicity.Effect: lead poisoning from fumes/handling (leaded types)

Notes: eutectic 63/37 Sn/Pb melts ~183C; modern lead-free uses Sn-Ag-Cu; composition nominal

Name: Pewter

Toxicity.PotencyNote: low (modern, lead-free); old pewter contained toxic lead

Notes: ~85-99% tin with copper/antimony; tableware; old pewter was lead-bearing; nominal composition

Name: Sterling silver
Notes: 92.5% silver + 7.5% copper; jewelry/tableware standard; composition nominal

Name: White gold

Toxicity.PotencyNote: low (bulk); nickel types can sensitize skin

Notes: gold alloyed with white metals (Ni or Pd) + often rhodium plated; nominal composition

Name: Gunmetal
Notes: 88/10/2 Cu/Sn/Zn; tough, corrosion-resistant; bearings, valves, fittings; nominal composition

Name: Nichrome
Notes: ~80/20 Ni/Cr; high resistivity + oxidation resistance for heating elements; nominal composition

Name: Duralumin
Notes: aluminium + ~4% Cu + Mg/Mn; strong and light, aircraft use; nominal composition

Name: Cupronickel
Notes: Cu + 10-30% Ni; seawater-corrosion-resistant; coinage, marine hardware; nominal composition

Name: Wood's metal

Toxicity.Effect: lead and cadmium poisoning hazard
Toxicity.Bioaccumulation: Pb in bone; Cd in kidney

Notes: eutectic melting ~70C; fusible links, sprinklers; toxic constituents; nominal composition

Name: Dental amalgam

Toxicity.PotencyNote: low when set; mercury vapor risk during placement/removal
Toxicity.Effect: mercury vapor exposure (neurotoxic) mainly during handling

Notes: ~50% mercury bound with silver-tin alloy; set amalgam releases minimal vapor; nominal composition

Name: Methane

Toxicity.Effect: displaces oxygen; explosive 5-15% in air

Notes: natural gas main component; potent greenhouse gas; density as gas at STP

Name: Ethane

Toxicity.Effect: oxygen displacement

Notes: natural gas component; feedstock for ethylene; density as gas at STP

Name: Propane

Toxicity.Effect: oxygen displacement; high concentration narcosis

Notes: LPG fuel; stored liquefied under pressure; density as gas at STP

Name: Butane

Toxicity.Effect: oxygen displacement; 'sudden sniffing death' from abuse

Notes: lighter fuel, camping stoves; density as gas at STP

Name: Ethylene

Toxicity.Effect: oxygen displacement; mild anesthetic

Notes: largest-volume organic chemical; plant ripening hormone; polyethylene monomer

Name: Acetylene

Toxicity.Effect: oxygen displacement

Notes: oxy-acetylene welding fuel; unstable under pressure (stored dissolved in acetone)

Name: Ethanol

Toxicity.Effect: CNS depression, intoxication; toxic in large doses

Notes: drinking alcohol; fuel, solvent, antiseptic, fermentation product

Name: Methanol

Toxicity.Effect: metabolized to formic acid → blindness, metabolic acidosis, death
Toxicity.AntidoteNote: fomepizole or ethanol; folinic acid; dialysis

Notes: wood alcohol; fuel/solvent/antifreeze; dangerous as a drinking-alcohol substitute

Name: Isopropanol

Toxicity.Effect: CNS depression (~2x ethanol potency), ketosis; gastritis

Notes: rubbing alcohol; solvent and disinfectant

Name: Acetone

Toxicity.Effect: CNS depression, mucous-membrane/skin irritation, defatting

Notes: nail-polish remover, solvent; a normal metabolic ketone

Name: Glucose
Notes: blood sugar; product of photosynthesis; central energy molecule

Name: Sucrose
Notes: table sugar; glucose + fructose disaccharide

Name: Cellulose
Notes: structural polysaccharide of plants; paper, cotton, wood; composition and molar mass are per glucose monomer unit (polymer has no single molar mass)

Name: Starch
Notes: plant energy-storage polysaccharide; food staple, adhesive, thickener; composition and molar mass are per glucose monomer unit

Name: Urea
Notes: protein-metabolism waste excreted in urine; major nitrogen fertilizer; first organic compound synthesized

Name: Glycerol
Notes: glycerin; humectant in food/cosmetics; nitroglycerin and soap precursor

Name: Benzene

Toxicity.Effect: bone-marrow toxicity, leukemia (chronic); CNS depression (acute)

Notes: aromatic feedstock; established human carcinogen; historic solvent now restricted

Name: Toluene

Toxicity.Effect: CNS depression, neurotoxicity with chronic abuse

Notes: solvent, octane booster, feedstock; less toxic than benzene

Name: Formaldehyde

Toxicity.Effect: airway/eye irritation; nasopharyngeal carcinogen; sensitizer

Notes: preservative (formalin is ~37% aqueous), resin and disinfectant; density as gas at STP

Name: Ethylene glycol

Toxicity.PotencyNote: toxic — sweet taste is a hazard
Toxicity.Effect: metabolized to oxalate → metabolic acidosis, renal failure

Notes: automotive antifreeze/coolant; dangerous to pets and children due to sweet taste

Name: Caffeine

Toxicity.PotencyNote: low at normal dose; toxic in grams
Toxicity.Effect: stimulant; high doses cause arrhythmia, seizures

Notes: CNS stimulant in coffee, tea, cocoa; purine alkaloid

Name: Ascorbic acid
Notes: vitamin C; antioxidant, antiscorbutic; deficiency causes scurvy

Name: Chloroform

Toxicity.Effect: CNS depression/anesthesia; hepatotoxic; probable carcinogen

Notes: former anesthetic and solvent; now restricted; forms in chlorinated water

Name: Diethyl ether

Toxicity.Effect: anesthesia, CNS depression

Notes: early surgical anesthetic, solvent; forms explosive peroxides on storage; extremely flammable

Name: Naphthalene

Toxicity.Effect: hemolytic anemia (esp. G6PD deficiency); possible carcinogen

Notes: mothballs; dye and resin feedstock; simplest polycyclic aromatic

Name: Phenol

Toxicity.Effect: caustic burns (often painless), systemic CNS/cardiac/renal toxicity

Notes: carbolic acid; first surgical antiseptic; resin and pharmaceutical feedstock

Name: Acetic anhydride

Toxicity.Effect: burns and lacrimation; reacts with moisture to acetic acid

Notes: acetylating agent (aspirin, cellulose acetate); watched chemical

Name: Ethyl acetate

Toxicity.Effect: irritation, CNS depression at high vapor levels

Notes: common solvent (nail polish, adhesives, decaf coffee); pleasant fruity smell

Name: Glycine
Notes: simplest amino acid; neurotransmitter, sweetener, buffer

Name: Nicotine
Appearance: colorless to pale yellow oily liquid (browns in air)

Toxicity.Effect: cholinergic stimulation then blockade; seizures, respiratory failure

Notes: tobacco alkaloid; stimulant and historic insecticide; readily absorbed through skin

Name: Hydrogen sulfide
Appearance: colorless gas, rotten-egg odor (deadens sense of smell)

Toxicity.Effect: inhibits cytochrome oxidase; rapid 'knockdown' and respiratory arrest at high ppm

Notes: sewer/swamp/volcanic gas, oil & gas hazard; olfactory fatigue makes high concentrations deceptively odorless

Name: Chlorine

Toxicity.Effect: pulmonary irritant/edema; eye and skin burns

Notes: water disinfection and bleaching; released by mixing bleach with acid/ammonia; first WWI war gas

Name: Phosgene

Toxicity.Effect: delayed pulmonary edema after a deceptive latent period
Toxicity.AntidoteNote: strict rest; oxygen; supportive (no specific antidote)

Notes: industrial intermediate (isocyanates, polycarbonates); insidious due to mild odor and delayed onset; historic war gas

Name: Sodium cyanide
Appearance: white deliquescent solid, faint almond odor

Toxicity.Effect: blocks cellular respiration; releases HCN gas with acid

Notes: gold mining (leaching), electroplating; deadly; never contact with acids

Name: Potassium cyanide
Appearance: white crystalline solid, faint almond odor

Toxicity.Effect: blocks cytochrome c oxidase → histotoxic hypoxia

Notes: electroplating, gold extraction, organic synthesis; among the fastest-acting poisons

Name: Arsenic trioxide

Toxicity.Effect: multi-organ failure, GI hemorrhage, cardiac, neuropathy; carcinogenic

Notes: 'white arsenic'; historic poison and pesticide; also a modern leukemia drug; tasteless makes it dangerous

Name: Lead(II) acetate

Toxicity.Effect: lead poisoning: neuro, hematologic, renal; sweet taste raises ingestion risk

Notes: 'sugar of lead'; historic sweetener (Roman) and pigment; now banned in most uses

Name: Mercury(II) chloride

Toxicity.Effect: corrosive GI injury; acute kidney failure; neuro/renal mercury toxicity

Notes: corrosive sublimate; historic disinfectant/preservative; soluble and highly toxic

Name: Tetraethyllead
Appearance: colorless to pale oily liquid, sweet odor

Toxicity.Effect: organic-lead neurotoxicity (psychosis, tremor); absorbed through skin
Toxicity.AntidoteNote: supportive; chelation of limited use for organolead

Notes: former anti-knock gasoline additive (leaded petrol); a major historic source of environmental lead

Name: Sodium azide

Toxicity.Effect: profound hypotension; blocks cytochrome oxidase (cyanide-like)

Notes: airbag gas generant, biocide/preservative; forms shock-sensitive heavy-metal azides with metals

Name: Hydrazine

Toxicity.Effect: CNS excitation/seizures, hepatotoxic, corrosive; suspected carcinogen

Notes: rocket propellant, blowing agent, oxygen scavenger; hypergolic with N2O4

Name: Methyl isocyanate

Toxicity.Effect: severe respiratory and eye injury, pulmonary edema

Notes: pesticide (carbaryl) intermediate; cause of the 1984 Bhopal disaster; reacts violently with water

Name: Carbon tetrachloride

Toxicity.Effect: severe hepatotoxicity/nephrotoxicity; CNS depression; probable carcinogen

Notes: former solvent/refrigerant/fire extinguisher; banned consumer use; ozone-depleting

Name: DDT

Toxicity.Effect: neurotoxin (tremors, seizures); bioaccumulates and biomagnifies
Toxicity.Bioaccumulation: strongly bioaccumulates in fat and up food chains

Notes: persistent organochlorine insecticide; banned in most uses; still used against malaria vectors

Name: Strychnine

Toxicity.Effect: glycine-receptor antagonist → violent convulsions, asphyxia

Notes: alkaloid from Strychnos seeds; historic rodenticide and poison

Name: Parathion
Appearance: pale yellow to brown liquid, garlic odor

Toxicity.Effect: organophosphate: irreversibly inhibits acetylcholinesterase → cholinergic crisis

Notes: banned/restricted organophosphate insecticide; readily absorbed through skin

Name: Uranium dioxide

Toxicity.Effect: heavy-metal kidney toxicity; alpha radiation hazard if inhaled as dust

Notes: primary nuclear reactor fuel (ceramic pellets); radioactivity from U isotopes (mainly U-238/U-235)

Name: Uranium hexafluoride

Toxicity.Effect: reacts with moisture to release corrosive HF + uranyl; HF burns and uranium nephrotoxicity

Notes: the uranium enrichment process gas (sublimes near 56C); double hazard from fluorine chemistry and uranium

Name: Triuranium octoxide

Toxicity.Effect: uranium heavy-metal toxicity; alpha hazard as dust

Notes: 'yellowcake' is largely U3O8; the most stable uranium oxide; milling/storage form

Name: Uranyl nitrate

Toxicity.Effect: soluble uranium kidney toxicity; radiological hazard

Notes: anhydrous form (molar mass computed from IUPAC standard atomic weights); soluble uranium salt; reprocessing and historic photographic toner; commonly handled as the hexahydrate

Name: Plutonium dioxide

Toxicity.Effect: inhaled fine particles deliver intense localized alpha dose → lung/bone cancer risk

Notes: reactor/weapon material and RTG fuel (Pu-238 form powers spacecraft); primarily an inhalation hazard

Name: Thorium dioxide

Toxicity.Effect: alpha emitter; historic Thorotrast contrast caused liver cancers

Notes: thoria; gas mantles, high-temperature ceramics; fertile thorium-fuel-cycle material (Th-232)

Name: Radium chloride

Toxicity.Effect: bone-seeker; alpha emitter causes bone necrosis/sarcoma (radium-dial painters)

Notes: historic luminous-paint and 'medical' source (Ra-226); first isolated by the Curies; intensely radioactive

Name: Americium dioxide

Toxicity.Effect: alpha hazard if inhaled/ingested; 59.5 keV gamma

Notes: Am-241 form is the ionization source in household smoke detectors (microgram quantities)

Name: Caesium-137 chloride

Toxicity.Effect: penetrating 662 keV gamma (from Ba-137m); whole-body dose; soluble → spreads in body and environment
Toxicity.Bioaccumulation: distributes like potassium in soft tissue

Notes: Cs-137 source form for radiotherapy/irradiators/gauges; the Goiania (1987) accident involved dispersed CsCl; ordinary (non-Cs-137) CsCl is not radioactive
